question: What is the thermal output of the NuScale Power Module (NPM)?

nuscale_ch01_0070.title: Legends and Symbols
nuscale_ch01_0070.summary: This section provides legends and symbols used in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report drawings, including detailed explanations for electrical diagram symbols such as transformers, circuit breakers, inverters, batteries, and other components. It clarifies the meaning of various notations and ratings associated with each symbol to ensure accurate interpretation of system drawings and diagrams.

nuscale_ch01_0052.title: Comparison with Other Facilities
nuscale_ch01_0052.summary: This section compares the major design features and parameters of the NuScale Power Plant with those of a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR). It provides a side-by-side summary of key characteristics such as electrical output, core design, reactor coolant system, and safety systems, highlighting the differences in scale, configuration, and safety components between NuScale’s modular design and conventional PWRs. The comparison is intended for reference and uses nominal values for both plant types.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, and waste management. It explains the distinction between certified and site-specific design elements, clarifies the use of representative conceptual design information (CDI), and notes that each COL applicant must reference and supplement the NuScale FSAR with site-specific details. Security-related information is specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0810.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.4 Detect and Suppress Method: Exclusion zone and buffer region methodology
nuscale_ch01_0810.summary: The NuScale design does not use the exclusion zone or buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability, as reactor trip signals are designed to prevent conditions that could lead to unstable flow oscillations. Instead, the existing reactor trip system (RTS) provides protection by tripping the reactor before safety limits are violated, making additional detect and suppress (D&S) methods unnecessary. Backup options and stability-related instrumentation for D&S are not applicable, as the RTS already ensures compliance with regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes special plant features of the NuScale Power Plant, highlighting structures such as the Central Utility Building and Diesel Generator Buildings, which house essential but nonsafety-related equipment and functions. It also emphasizes human factors engineering, noting that the plant design minimizes human error through automation, digital controls, and optimized workload management, in compliance with regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0811.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.5 Detect and Suppress Method Trip of reactor before SAFDL violation
nuscale_ch01_0811.summary: The NuScale design does not use exclusion zone or buffer region methodologies for thermal hydraulic stability, relying instead on existing reactor trip signals to prevent violation of safety limits before unstable flow oscillations can develop. The design partially conforms to regulatory criteria by providing more limiting protection than required, but does not employ detect and suppress systems or backup options, as reactor trips occur before instability conditions arise. Compliance with GDC 20 is partially met, as the system prevents instability but does not actively detect or suppress it.

nuscale_ch01_0018.title: Principal Design Criteria
nuscale_ch01_0018.summary: The Principal Design Criteria section describes the NuScale plant’s cooling and power generation systems. It outlines how the reactor core heats coolant, which circulates naturally to transfer heat to steam generators, producing superheated steam that drives a turbine and generates electricity. The section also notes that site-specific details apply to cooling tower design and operation, with further information provided elsewhere in the report.

nuscale_ch01_0017.title: Facility Description
nuscale_ch01_0017.summary: The Facility Description section outlines the NuScale plant’s cooling and power generation processes. It explains that the reactor core heats coolant, which circulates naturally to transfer heat to steam generators, producing superheated steam that drives a turbine and generates electricity. The section also notes that site-specific details exist for cooling tower design and operation, and that the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) supports auxiliary systems.

nuscale_ch01_0006.title: Power Output
nuscale_ch01_0006.summary: The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each rated at 160 MWt and producing approximately 50 MWe, for a total net output of about 570 MWe when accounting for house loads. The electrical output may vary depending on environmental conditions, and the design includes a 2% margin for measurement uncertainty.

nuscale_ch01_0007.title: Schedule
nuscale_ch01_0007.summary: This section describes the NuScale containment vessel as a cylindrical, steel, ASME-certified pressure vessel designed to withstand high-pressure events, with internal vacuum pressure during normal operations. The NuScale reactor is a passive, small modular pressurized water reactor with integrated components and natural circulation, eliminating the need for external piping and coolant pumps. The plant can consist of up to 12 modules, each producing 50 MWe, for a total net output of approximately 570 MWe, and applicants referencing this design must provide their own project schedules.
